questions.question: According to Flow Theory (Csikszentmihalyi), when do flow states most reliably occur in games and puzzles?
options: When the activity is extremely easy and requires no effort
When challenge level slightly exceeds current skill (balanced challenge-skill ratio), with clear goals and immediate feedback
Only when playing competitively for money or status
Flow states never occur during recreational activities
questions.explanation: Flow Theory predicts that flow states occur most reliably when challenge slightly exceeds current skill (stretch without overwhelm), with clear goals, immediate feedback, and intrinsic motivation. Too easy = boredom; too hard = anxiety. Games naturally create these optimal challenge conditions when difficulty matches your skill level.

questions.question: What does longitudinal research show about cognitively engaging leisure activities (puzzles, games, learning)?
options: Cognitively stimulating leisure has no effect on brain health
Cognitive leisure is associated with cognitive preservation, supports cognitive reserve, and is linked to lower dementia risk in longitudinal studies
Only physical exercise supports brain health
Games cause cognitive decline
questions.explanation: Research shows cognitively stimulating leisure activities (puzzles, strategy games, learning) are associated with cognitive preservation and support cognitive reserve — the brain's resilience against age-related decline. Longitudinal studies link cognitive leisure to lower dementia risk. Mental engagement through games provides real cognitive benefits.

questions.question: How do cooperative games (Pandemic, escape rooms, team challenges) differ from competitive games in their psychological benefits?
options: Cooperative games provide no benefits
Cooperative games provide team bonding without competition stress (everyone wins/loses together), reduce performance anxiety through shared responsibility, and support collaborative problem-solving
Competitive games are always superior
There is no difference between cooperative and competitive formats
questions.explanation: Cooperative games offer distinct benefits: team bonding without competitive stress (everyone wins or loses together, not against each other), reduced performance anxiety (shared responsibility vs. individual failure), and collaborative problem-solving practice. This format particularly benefits people who want social gaming without competitive pressure.

questions.question: What is the key difference between healthy recreational gaming and Gaming Disorder (WHO ICD-11)?
options: There is no difference — all gaming is unhealthy

Recreational gaming is freely chosen, time-bounded, and balanced with other activities; Gaming Disorder involves loss of control, functional impairment (work/relationships/self-care suffer), and continuation despite negative consequences
Anyone who plays games daily has Gaming Disorder
Gaming Disorder only affects children
questions.explanation: Healthy recreational gaming is freely chosen, time-bounded, balanced with other life activities, and enhances overall functioning. Gaming Disorder involves loss of control over gaming, prioritizing gaming over essential activities to the point of significant impairment, and continuing despite negative consequences. Frequency alone doesn't determine disorder — functional impairment and loss of control do.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what are effective solutions for the barrier 'I'm not good at games / I always lose'?
options: Avoid all games forever
Choose solo games/puzzles (compete with yourself), cooperative games (everyone wins/loses together), adjustable difficulty settings, or beginner-friendly groups that welcome newcomers
Only play games if you're guaranteed to win
Performance in games determines your worth as a person
questions.explanation: Performance anxiety in gaming can be addressed by choosing formats that reduce social comparison: solo games/puzzles (compete with yourself or the puzzle), cooperative games (shared outcomes), adjustable difficulty (match your skill level), or explicitly beginner-friendly groups that prioritize learning over winning. The goal is engagement and enjoyment, not perfect performance.
